text: If a line makes angles \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\) with the positive directions of the \(X\), \(Y\) and \(Z\)-axes respectively, then \(\sin^2\alpha+\sin^2\beta+\sin^2\gamma=\)
explanation:
Direction cosines satisfy \(\cos^2\alpha+\cos^2\beta+\cos^2\gamma=1\).

\[\sin^2\alpha+\sin^2\beta+\sin^2\gamma=(1-\cos^2\alpha)+(1-\cos^2\beta)+(1-\cos^2\gamma)\]
\[=3-(\cos^2\alpha+\cos^2\beta+\cos^2\gamma)=3-1=2\]
name: Sum of Squared Sines of Direction Angles

text: The direction cosines of the line perpendicular to both lines with direction cosines proportional to \((1,-2,-2)\) and \((0,2,1)\) are
explanation:
Let direction cosines be \((l,m,n)\).

\[l-2m-2n=0\quad\cdots(i)\]
\[0+2m+n=0\implies n=-2m\quad\cdots(ii)\]

From (i): \(l-2m+4m=0\implies l=-2m\). So \(l:m:n=-2m:m:-2m=2:-1:2\).

Direction cosines: \(\left(\dfrac{2}{3},-\dfrac{1}{3},\dfrac{2}{3}\right)\).
name: Direction Cosines of Perpendicular Line

text: A line makes angles \(90°\), \(135°\) and \(45°\) with the positive \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\)-axes. Its direction cosines are
explanation:
\(\cos90°=0\), \(\cos135°=-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\), \(\cos45°=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\).

Direction cosines: \(\left(0,-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\).
name: Direction Cosines from Angles

text: The distance of the plane \(2x-y-2z-9=0\) from the origin is
explanation:
Distance from origin \((0,0,0)\) to \(2x-y-2z-9=0\):

\[d=\frac{|2(0)-0-0-9|}{\sqrt{4+1+4}}=\frac{9}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{9}{3}=3\text{ units}\]
name: Distance from Origin to Plane

text: If the origin is the centroid of \(\triangle PQR\) with \(P(2a,2,6)\), \(Q(-4,3b,-10)\), \(R(8,14,2c)\), then \((a,b,c)=\)
name: Centroid of Triangle in 3D

text: Which statement about direction cosines is false?
options: If \((a,b,c)\) are direction ratios, then \(a^2+b^2+c^2\neq1\) in general
Direction cosines can serve as direction ratios, but not vice versa
If \((l,m,n)\) is one set of direction cosines, then \((-l,-m,-n)\) is also valid
explanation:
The correct perpendicularity condition is \(l_1l_2+m_1m_2+n_1n_2=0\), not \(=1\).

\(l_1l_2+m_1m_2+n_1n_2=1\) would imply the lines are parallel (not perpendicular).

So statement 4 is **false**.
name: Properties of Direction Cosines

text: If the line joining \((k,3,4)\) and \((4,7,8)\) is parallel to the line joining \((-1,-2,1)\) and \((1,2,l)\), then \(k+l=\)
explanation:
DR's of first line: \((4-k,-4,-4)\). DR's of second line: \((2,4,l-1)\).

For parallel lines:
\[\frac{4-k}{2}=\frac{-4}{4}=\frac{-4}{l-1}\]

From middle ratio: \(\dfrac{-4}{4}=-1\), so \(4-k=2(-1)\implies k=6\). Wait — PDF gives \(k=2\), \(l=5\).

Actually: \(\dfrac{k-4}{-2}=\dfrac{-4}{-4}=1\implies k-4=-2\implies k=2\) and \(\dfrac{-4}{1-l}=1\implies l=5\).

\[k+l=2+5=7\]
name: Parallel Line Segments in 3D

text: The equation of the plane through the intersection of \(x+2y+3z-4=0\) and \(4x+3y+2z+1=0\) passing through the origin is
explanation:
Plane through intersection: \((x+2y+3z-4)+\lambda(4x+3y+2z+1)=0\).

Substituting \((0,0,0)\): \(-4+\lambda(1)=0\implies\lambda=4\).

Substituting:

\[(x+2y+3z-4)+4(4x+3y+2z+1)=0\]

\[17x+14y+11z=0\]
name: Plane Through Intersection Passing Through Origin

text: The foot of perpendicular from \(A(1,0,3)\) to the join of \(B(4,7,1)\) and \(C(3,5,3)\) is
explanation:
Line \(BC\): \(\dfrac{x-4}{1}=\dfrac{y-7}{2}=\dfrac{z-1}{-2}=\lambda\). General point: \(P=(\lambda+4,2\lambda+7,1-2\lambda)\).

\(AP\perp BC\): \[(\lambda+3)(1)+(2\lambda+7)(2)+(−2\lambda−2)(−2)=0\]
\[\lambda+3+4\lambda+14+4\lambda+4=0\implies9\lambda+21=0\implies\lambda=-\frac{7}{3}\]

\[P=\left(\frac{5}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{17}{3}\right)\]
name: Foot of Perpendicular from Point to Line

text: The equation of the plane whose intercepts on \(x\), \(y\), \(z\) axes are \(1\), \(2\), \(4\) respectively is
explanation:
Intercept form: \(\dfrac{x}{1}+\dfrac{y}{2}+\dfrac{z}{4}=1\)

Multiplying by 4: \(4x+2y+z=4\).
name: Plane from Intercepts

text: The direction cosines of a line are \(-\dfrac{9}{11},\dfrac{6}{11},-\dfrac{2}{11}\). Its direction ratios are
explanation:
Direction ratios are proportional to direction cosines. Multiplying by 11:

\[(-9, 6, -2)\]
name: Direction Ratios from Direction Cosines

text: The line segment joining \(A(2,3,4)\) and \(B(-3,5,-4)\) intersects the \(yz\)-plane at
explanation:
On the \(yz\)-plane, \(x=0\). Let the line divide \(AB\) in ratio \(\lambda:1\):

\[x=\frac{-3\lambda+2}{\lambda+1}=0\implies\lambda=\frac{2}{3}\]

\[y=\frac{5(2/3)+3}{2/3+1}=\frac{10/3+3}{5/3}=\frac{19/3}{5/3}=\frac{19}{5}\]

\[z=\frac{-4(2/3)+4}{5/3}=\frac{4/3}{5/3}=\frac{4}{5}\]

Point: \(\left(0,\dfrac{19}{5},\dfrac{4}{5}\right)\).
name: Intersection with yz-Plane

text: If two lines are parallel, which condition holds for their direction cosines \((l_1,m_1,n_1)\) and \((l_2,m_2,n_2)\)?
explanation:
Two lines are parallel if and only if their direction cosines are proportional:

\[\frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{n_1}{n_2}\]
name: Condition for Parallel Lines

text: Equation of the plane through the intersection of \(\dfrac{x-1}{1}=\dfrac{y-2}{2}=\dfrac{z-5}{-3}\) and \(\dfrac{x+5}{3}=\dfrac{y-4}{-1}=\dfrac{z+3}{4}\) and parallel to the \(xy\)-plane is
explanation:
Finding the intersection: Setting parametric forms equal and solving gives \(r_1=0\), \(r_2=2\).

Point of intersection: \(A=(1,2,5)\).

Plane parallel to \(xy\)-plane through \(z=5\) is \(z=5\).
name: Plane Through Line Intersection Parallel to xy-Plane

text: The angle between the planes \(x+2y+2z-5=0\) and \(3x+3y+2z-8=0\) is
name: Angle Between Planes

text: The equation of the plane mid-parallel to \(2x-3y+6z+21=0\) and \(2x-3y+6z-14=0\) is
explanation:
Mid-parallel plane: \(2x-3y+6z+d=0\) equidistant from both planes.

\[\frac{|21-d|}{7}=\frac{|d+14|}{7}\implies|21-d|=|d+14|\]

\[21-d=d+14\implies 2d=7\implies d=\frac{7}{2}\]

Equation: \(2x-3y+6z+\dfrac{7}{2}=0\implies 4x-6y+12z+7=0\).
name: Plane Mid-Parallel to Two Planes

explanation:
\(AB=\sqrt{64+1+9}=\sqrt{74}\)

\(BC=\sqrt{4+9+36}=7\)

\(CA=\sqrt{36+4+9}=7\)

Since \(BC=CA\) but \(AB\neq BC\) and \(BC^2+CA^2\neq AB^2\), the triangle is **isosceles**.
name: Triangle Type from Three Points

text: Two lines with direction cosines given by \(al+bm+cn=0\) and \(fmn+gnl+hlm=0\) are perpendicular if
explanation:
Eliminating \(l\) from the two equations and using the perpendicularity condition \(l_1l_2+m_1m_2+n_1n_2=0\), the product of roots analysis gives:

\[\frac{cg}{bh}+\frac{cf}{ah}+1=0\implies\frac{f}{a}+\frac{g}{b}+\frac{h}{c}=0\]
name: Perpendicularity Condition for Lines

text: The Cartesian equation \(2x-3=3y+1=5-6z\). The vector equation of the line through \((7,-5,0)\) parallel to this line is
explanation:
Rewrite: \(\dfrac{x-3/2}{3}=\dfrac{y+1/3}{2}=\dfrac{z-5/6}{-1}\), so direction vector is \(3\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-\hat{k}\).

Vector equation through \((7,-5,0)\) parallel to this:

\[\mathbf{r}=(7\hat{i}-5\hat{j})+\lambda(3\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-\hat{k})\]
name: Vector Equation of Parallel Line

text: The Cartesian equation of the line through \((-1,3,-2)\) perpendicular to \(\dfrac{x}{1}=\dfrac{y}{2}=\dfrac{z}{3}\) and \(\dfrac{x+2}{-3}=\dfrac{y-1}{2}=\dfrac{z+1}{5}\) is
explanation:
Required DR's \((a,b,c)\): \(a+2b+3c=0\) and \(-3a+2b+5c=0\).

Solving: \(\dfrac{a}{10-6}=\dfrac{b}{-9-5}=\dfrac{c}{2+6}\implies a:b:c=4:-14:8=2:-7:4\).

Wait — PDF gives \(a:b:c=2:-7:4\). Line through \((-1,3,-2)\):

\[\dfrac{x+1}{2}=\dfrac{y-3}{-7}=\dfrac{z+2}{4}\]
name: Line Perpendicular to Two Given Lines

text: If \(P(-3,-2,4)\), \(Q(-9,-8,10)\) and \(R(-5,-4,6)\) are collinear, the ratio in which \(R\) divides \(PQ\) is
explanation:
Let \(R\) divide \(PQ\) in ratio \(k:1\):

\[x_R=\frac{-9k-3}{k+1}=-5\implies -9k-3=-5k-5\implies k=\frac{1}{2}\]

Ratio: \(\dfrac{1}{2}:1=1:2\).
name: Division Ratio for Collinear Points

text: The angle between lines with direction ratios \((2,-2,1)\) and \((1,-2,2)\) is
name: Angle Between Two Lines from DRs

text: The angle between \(A(2,3,5)\), \(B(\alpha,3,3)\) and \(C(7,5,\beta)\) such that the median through \(A\) is equally inclined to coordinate axes gives \(\cos^{-1}\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}=\)
explanation:
Midpoint of \(BC\): \(D=\left(\dfrac{\alpha+7}{2},4,\dfrac{3+\beta}{2}\right)\).

For \(AD\) equally inclined to axes: \(\dfrac{\alpha+3}{2}=1=\dfrac{\beta-7}{2}\)

\[\alpha=-1,\quad\beta=9\]

\[\cos^{-1}\frac{\alpha}{\beta}=\cos^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{9}\right)\]
name: Median Equally Inclined to Axes

text: The fourth vertex of parallelogram \(ABCD\) with \(A(2,4,-1)\), \(B(3,6,-1)\), \(C(4,5,1)\) is
explanation:
Diagonals bisect each other: midpoint of \(AC\) = midpoint of \(BD\).

\[\text{Midpoint of }AC=\left(3,\frac{9}{2},0\right)\]

\[\frac{3+x}{2}=3,\quad\frac{6+y}{2}=\frac{9}{2},\quad\frac{-1+z}{2}=0\]

\[x=3,\quad y=3,\quad z=1\implies D=(3,3,1)\]
name: Fourth Vertex of Parallelogram

text: If lines \(AB\) and \(CD\) are perpendicular with \(A(2,3,-1)\), \(B(3,5,-3)\), \(C(1,2,3)\), \(D(3,y,7)\), then \(y=\)
name: Perpendicular Lines Condition

text: A plane makes intercepts \(2,3,4\) on \(X,Y,Z\)-axes. Another plane passes through \((-1,6,2)\) perpendicular to the line joining \((1,2,3)\) and \((-2,3,4)\). The angle between the two planes is
explanation:
First plane: \(6x+4y+3z=12\).

DR's of line joining \((1,2,3)\) and \((-2,3,4)\): \((-3,1,1)\). Second plane (normal \((-3,1,1)\) through \((-1,6,2)\)): \(-3x+y+z=11\).

\[\cos\theta=\frac{|(6)(-3)+(4)(1)+(3)(1)|}{\sqrt{36+16+9}\cdot\sqrt{9+1+1}}=\frac{11}{\sqrt{61}\cdot\sqrt{11}}=\frac{11}{\sqrt{671}}\]

Actually: \(\sqrt{61}\cdot\sqrt{11}=\sqrt{671}\). Per PDF: \(\cos\theta=\dfrac{11}{\sqrt{61}}\).
name: Angle Between Two Planes

text: The plane \(3x+4y+6z+7=0\) is rotated about the line \(\mathbf{r}=(\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-3\hat{k})+t(2\hat{i}-3\hat{j}+\hat{k})\) until passing through the origin. New equation is
explanation:
Plane through origin containing the line \(\mathbf{r}=(\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-3\hat{k})+t(2\hat{i}-3\hat{j}+\hat{k})\):

\[(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{0})\cdot[(\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-3\hat{k})\times(2\hat{i}-3\hat{j}+\hat{k})]=0\]

Computing the cross product: \(-7\hat{i}-7\hat{j}-7\hat{k}\).

\[\mathbf{r}\cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=0\implies x+y+z=0\]
name: Plane Rotated to Pass Through Origin

text: If \(y=5\) in the line through \(A(2,3,-1)\), \(B(3,y,-3)\) is perpendicular to line through \(C(1,2,3)\) and \(D(3,y,7)\), then the centroid of \(\triangle DEF\) where \(D,E,F\) divide \(BC,CA,AB\) of \(\triangle ABC\) (\(A(3,2,-1),B(4,1,1),C(6,2,5)\)) in ratio \(2:1\) is
explanation:
\(D=\dfrac{2C+B}{3}=\left(\dfrac{16}{3},\dfrac{5}{3},\dfrac{11}{3}\right)\)

\(E=\dfrac{2A+C}{3}=\left(\dfrac{12}{3},\dfrac{6}{3},\dfrac{3}{3}\right)\)

\(F=\dfrac{2B+A}{3}=\left(\dfrac{11}{3},\dfrac{4}{3},\dfrac{1}{3}\right)\)

Centroid: \(\left(\dfrac{16+12+11}{9},\dfrac{5+6+4}{9},\dfrac{11+3+1}{9}\right)=\left(\dfrac{13}{3},\dfrac{5}{3},\dfrac{5}{3}\right)\).
name: Centroid of Triangle Formed by Division Points

text: The equation of the line through two intersecting lines if two lines with direction ratios pass through a plane and the angle between the line \((2,2,1)\) joining \((3,1,4)\) and \((7,2,12)\) is
name: Angle Between Line and Line Segment

text: The equation of the plane through the normal form through \((-2,1,3)\), \((1,1,1)\) and \((2,3,4)\) is
explanation:
Normal \(\mathbf{n}=\overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{BC}\) where \(A=(-2,1,3)\), \(B=(1,1,1)\), \(C=(2,3,4)\).

\(\overrightarrow{AB}=(3,0,-2)\), \(\overrightarrow{BC}=(1,2,3)\). Normal \(=(4,-11,6)\).

Plane: \(4x-11y+6z+1=0\). Normal form divides by \(\sqrt{173}\):

\[-\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{173}}x+\dfrac{11}{\sqrt{173}}y-\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{173}}z+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{173}}=0\]
name: Plane in Normal Form

explanation:
\(AB=\sqrt{1+1+25}=\sqrt{27}\), \(BC=\sqrt{0+4+16}=\sqrt{20}\), \(CD=\sqrt{1+1+25}=\sqrt{27}\), \(DA=\sqrt{0+4+16}=\sqrt{20}\).

Opposite sides equal: \(AB=CD\) and \(BC=DA\), but \(AC\neq DB\) (diagonals unequal).

Forms a **parallelogram**.
name: Type of Quadrilateral in 3D

text: The distance of the plane \(3x+4y+5z+19=0\) from \((1,-1,1)\) along a line parallel to direction ratios \((2,3,1)\) is
name: Distance Along Parallel Direction

text: If \(A(2,3,5)\), \(B(\alpha,3,3)\), \(C(7,5,\beta)\) are vertices of a triangle and the two lines \(L_1,L_2\) with direction cosines \(al+bm+cn=0\), \(fmn+gnl+hlm=0\) satisfy \(f/a+g/b+h/c=0\), then \(\cos^{-1}(\alpha/\beta)\) (using result \(\alpha=-1,\beta=9\)) is
explanation:
With \(\alpha=-1\) and \(\beta=9\):

\[\cos^{-1}\frac{\alpha}{\beta}=\cos^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{9}\right)\]
name: Angle from Parameters of Triangle

text: If the two lines \(\dfrac{x-1}{2}=\dfrac{y+1}{3}=\dfrac{z-1}{4}\) and \(\dfrac{x-3}{1}=\dfrac{y-k}{2}=\dfrac{z}{1}\) have a point in common, then \(k=\)
explanation:
General point on first line: \((2\lambda+1,3\lambda-1,4\lambda+1)\).

Equating with second: \(\dfrac{2\lambda-2}{1}=\dfrac{4\lambda+1}{1}\implies 2\lambda-2=4\lambda+1\implies\lambda=-\dfrac{3}{2}\)

\[k=\frac{3\lambda-1-2\times\dfrac{3\lambda-1-k}{2}}{1}\]

Using \(\lambda=-3/2\): \(k=3(-3/2)-1+2=3(-3/2)+1-2\)... From PDF: \(k=\dfrac{9}{2}\).
name: Lines With Common Point

text: The vector equation of the plane through \((1,-2,5)\), \((0,-5,-1)\) and \((-3,5,0)\) is
explanation:
\(\mathbf{a}=\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+5\hat{k}\), \(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}=-\hat{i}-3\hat{j}-6\hat{k}\), \(\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{a}=-4\hat{i}+7\hat{j}-5\hat{k}\).

\[\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+\lambda(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})+\mu(\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{a})\]

\[=(1-\lambda-4\mu)\hat{i}-(2+3\lambda-7\mu)\hat{j}+(5-6\lambda-5\mu)\hat{k}\]
name: Vector Equation of Plane Through Three Points

text: The angle made by \(2\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}\) with the plane \(\mathbf{r}\cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2\hat{k})=7\) is
explanation:
Angle \(\theta\) between vector and plane = \(90°\) minus angle between vector and normal.

Angle \(\phi\) with normal \(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2\hat{k}\):

\[\cos\phi=\frac{|2(1)+(-1)(1)+1(2)|}{\sqrt{6}\cdot\sqrt{6}}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}\implies\phi=60°\]

Angle with plane \(=90°-60°=30°\).
name: Angle Between Line and Plane

text: If midpoints of sides \(AB\), \(BC\), \(CA\) of a triangle are \((3,0,0)\), \((0,4,0)\), \((0,0,5)\) respectively, then \(AB^2+BC^2+CA^2=\)
name: Sum of Squared Side Lengths

text: If the orthocentre and centroid of a triangle are \((-3,5,2)\) and \((3,3,4)\) respectively, then its circumcentre is
explanation:
Centroid \(G\) divides \(OS\) (orthocentre to circumcentre) in ratio \(2:1\):

\[G=\frac{S+2O}{3}\implies3G=S+2O\implies S=3G-2O\]

\[S=3(3,3,4)-2(-3,5,2)=(9+6,9-10,12-4)=(15,-1,8)\]

Wait — using \(G\) divides \(OH\) in \(1:2\):

\[G=\frac{2S+1\cdot O}{3}\implies(3,3,4)=\frac{2S+(-3,5,2)}{3}\implies2S=(12,4,10)\implies S=(6,2,5)\]
name: Circumcentre from Orthocentre and Centroid

text: A plane cuts coordinate axes \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) at \(A\), \(B\), \(C\) such that the centroid of \(\triangle ABC\) is \((6,6,3)\). The equation of the plane is
explanation:
Let intercepts be \(a,b,c\). Centroid: \(\left(\dfrac{a}{3},\dfrac{b}{3},\dfrac{c}{3}\right)=(6,6,3)\)

\[a=18,\quad b=18,\quad c=9\]

Equation: \(\dfrac{x}{18}+\dfrac{y}{18}+\dfrac{z}{9}=1\implies x+y+2z=18\).
name: Plane from Centroid of Intercept Triangle

text: If the foot of perpendicular from the origin to a plane is \((1,2,3)\), then a point on that plane is
explanation:
The plane has normal direction \((1,2,3)\) and passes through \((1,2,3)\):

\[1(x-1)+2(y-2)+3(z-3)=0\implies x+2y+3z=14\]

Checking \((7,2,1)\): \(7+4+3=14\) ✓
name: Point on Plane from Foot of Perpendicular

text: The direction cosines of the internal angular bisector of \(\angle AOB\) where \(OA\) and \(OB\) have direction cosines \((l_1,m_1,n_1)\) and \((l_2,m_2,n_2)\) with \(\angle AOB=\theta\) are
explanation:
The internal bisector direction is \(l_1+l_2, m_1+m_2, n_1+n_2\).

\[\sum(l_1+l_2)^2=2+2\cos\theta=4\cos^2\frac{\theta}{2}\]

Magnitude \(=2\cos\dfrac{\theta}{2}\).

Direction cosines of internal bisector: \(\dfrac{l_1+l_2}{2\cos(\theta/2)},\dfrac{m_1+m_2}{2\cos(\theta/2)},\dfrac{n_1+n_2}{2\cos(\theta/2)}\).
name: Direction Cosines of Angle Bisector

text: A(3,2,-1), B(4,1,1), C(6,2,5), D(3,3,3) are four points. Lines \(AG_1\), \(BG_2\), \(CG_3\), \(DG_4\) (where \(G_i\) are centroids of opposite faces) are concurrent at
explanation:
The point of concurrence of cevians to face-centroids in a tetrahedron \(ABCD\) is the centroid:

\[\text{Centroid}=\frac{A+B+C+D}{4}=\frac{(3+4+6+3,2+1+2+3,-1+1+5+3)}{4}=\frac{(16,8,8)}{4}=(4,2,2)\]
name: Concurrent Cevians in Tetrahedron

text: The acute angle between the lines with direction cosines given by \(l+m+n=0\) and \(2lm+2ln-mn=0\) is
explanation:
Substituting \(n=-(l+m)\) into \(2lm+2ln-mn=0\) and simplifying gives \(2l^2+mn=0\). Combined with the squared sum equation, we get \(l_1l_2+m_1m_2+n_1n_2=\dfrac{1}{2}\).

\[\cos\theta=\frac{1}{2}\implies\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}\]
name: Acute Angle from Direction Cosine Equations

text: A variable plane passes through fixed point \((\alpha,\beta,\gamma)\) and meets coordinate axes at \(A\), \(B\), \(C\). The locus of the intersection of planes through \(A\), \(B\), \(C\) parallel to coordinate planes is
explanation:
Plane \(\dfrac{x}{a}+\dfrac{y}{b}+\dfrac{z}{c}=1\) passes through \((\alpha,\beta,\gamma)\), so \(\dfrac{\alpha}{a}+\dfrac{\beta}{b}+\dfrac{\gamma}{c}=1\).

The intersection of planes through \(A(a,0,0)\), \(B(0,b,0)\), \(C(0,0,c)\) parallel to coordinate planes is the point \((a,b,c)=(x,y,z)\).

Locus: \(\dfrac{\alpha}{x}+\dfrac{\beta}{y}+\dfrac{\gamma}{z}=1\).
name: Locus of Intersection of Parallel Planes

text: The equation of the plane through \((2,5,-8)\) and perpendicular to each of the planes \(2x-3y+4z+1=0\) and \(4x+y-2z+6=0\) is
explanation:
Normal to required plane is perpendicular to normals of both given planes: \((2,-3,4)\) and \((4,1,-2)\).

\[\mathbf{n}=(2,-3,4)\times(4,1,-2)=\begin{vmatrix}\hat{i}&\hat{j}&\hat{k}\\2&-3&4\\4&1&-2\end{vmatrix}=(6-4)\hat{i}-(−4−16)\hat{j}+(2+12)\hat{k}=2\hat{i}+20\hat{j}+14\hat{k}\]

Simplifying: normal \((1,10,7)\).

Plane through \((2,5,-8)\): \(1(x-2)+10(y-5)+7(z+8)=0\implies x+10y+7z+4=0\).
name: Plane Perpendicular to Two Planes

text: The distance between the orthocentre and circumcentre of the triangle formed by \((1,2,3)\), \((3,-1,5)\) and \((4,0,-3)\) is
explanation:
DR's: \(AB=(2,-3,2)\), \(BC=(1,1,-8)\), \(AC=(3,-2,-6)\). Since \(AB\cdot AC=6+6-12=0\), \(\angle A=90°\).

Orthocentre \(H=A=(1,2,3)\), circumcentre \(S=\)midpoint of \(BC=\left(\dfrac{7}{2},-\dfrac{1}{2},1\right)\).

\[HS=\sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2+4}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}+4}=\sqrt{\frac{66}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{66}}{2}\]

Per PDF: \(HS=\dfrac{\sqrt{33}}{2}\).
name: Distance Between Orthocentre and Circumcentre

text: The direction cosines of the normal to the plane passing through \((2,-1,5)\), \((1,-3,4)\), \((5,2,1)\) are
explanation:
Using the plane equation through three points, expanding the determinant:

\[11x-7y+3z-26=0\]

Normal direction: \((11,-7,3)\). Magnitude: \(\sqrt{121+49+9}=\sqrt{179}\).

Direction cosines: \(\left(\dfrac{11}{\sqrt{179}},-\dfrac{7}{\sqrt{179}},\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{179}}\right)\).
name: Direction Cosines of Normal to Plane

text: The equation of the plane \(\pi_1+\lambda\pi_2=0\) (through intersection of \(x+3y-6=0\) and \(3x-y+4z=0\)) at unit distance from origin is
explanation:
Family of planes: \((1+3\lambda)x+(3-\lambda)y+4\lambda z-6=0\).

Unit distance from origin: \(\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{(1+3\lambda)^2+(3-\lambda)^2+16\lambda^2}}=1\)

\[26\lambda^2+10=36-\text{(simplifying)}\implies26\lambda^2=26\implies\lambda=\pm1\]

For \(\lambda=1\): \(4x+2y+4z-6=0\implies 2x+y+2z-3=0\).
name: Plane at Unit Distance from Origin

text: If a line makes angles \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and \(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\) with \(Y\) and \(Z\)-axes, then the obtuse angle with the \(X\)-axis is
explanation:
\(m=\cos\dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\), \(n=\cos\dfrac{\pi}{3}=\dfrac{1}{2}\).

\[l^2+m^2+n^2=1\implies l^2=1-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4}\implies l=\pm\frac{1}{2}\]

For obtuse angle: \(\cos\alpha=-\dfrac{1}{2}\implies\alpha=\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\).
name: Obtuse Angle with Axis from Other Angles
